title: The Innocent Quiz
introduction: Read this social media interaction. Riley comes across a post and starts engaging with it. Tap anything you think is a warning sign, then press Submit.

script.text:
🎉 GIVEAWAY TIME! We're giving away €500 in cash to 3 lucky winners!

To enter:
✅ Like this post
✅ Share to your timeline
✅ Comment your full name and date of birth below

Winner announced Friday! Good luck! 🍀
script.explanation: Legitimate competitions never need your date of birth in a public comment. Your full name + date of birth is a key combination for identity theft and account recovery attacks. This post is harvesting personal data from thousands of people at once.

script.text: Winners will be announced live on our Facebook page this Friday at 6pm.

DM from 'CompetitionHQ': Congratulations Riley! You're one of our winners 🎉 To claim your €500 please verify your identity by providing: your address, mobile number, and the last 4 digits of your bank card.
script.explanation: The 'you've won' DM following a public post is a second-stage harvest. Riley has already given their name and DOB. Now the scammer is building a complete profile: address + mobile + card digits. Together with publicly available info, this is enough to attempt account takeovers or fraud.

script.text: DM from 'CompetitionHQ': Thanks for entering! We've been running this draw for three years.
Oh amazing! Sure — [address], 087-XXX-XXXX, and the last 4 are 4821.
DM from 'CompetitionHQ': Perfect, thanks! Just one last step — there's a small €2.99 processing fee to release the prize. You can pay via this link: prize-release-payment.com/verify
script.explanation: The 'small fee to release your prize' is the final step. Real competitions have no fees — ever. This is both a direct money grab and a way to capture full card details. Riley never won anything. There is no €500.

script.text:
SEPARATE POST — 'Fun Quiz: What's your personality type?'

Answer these to find out!
🔹 What street did you grow up on?
🔹 What was your first pet's name?
🔹 What's your mother's maiden name?
🔹 What was your first car?
script.explanation: These four questions are almost universal bank security questions. A quiz that asks exactly these things — no matter how fun the framing — is harvesting the answers to your account recovery questions. Never answer these publicly.

learning_objectives: Never post your full name and date of birth publicly
Real competitions never charge a fee to claim a prize
Fun quizzes asking for street names, pets, and mother's maiden name are harvesting security question answers
The combination of name + DOB + address + card digits enables identity fraud
If you've already shared this data, change your security questions and alert your bank
debrief:
This scenario shows two related identity theft techniques — fake giveaways and security question harvesting quizzes — both extremely common on Facebook and Instagram.

**What makes this effective:** Each step seems harmless. Your name and birthday in a comment. Answering a fun quiz. A tiny €2.99 fee. None of it screams 'danger' in the moment.

**What actually happened:** A complete profile was assembled — name, DOB, address, phone, partial card number, security question answers. That's enough to attempt account takeovers on banks, email providers, and social platforms.

**The rule:** If a social media post asks for personal information as part of a competition, quiz, or giveaway — don't. Real prizes don't need your mother's maiden name.
